The transition is expensive, and its costs are steepest in the early stages. Recently settled individuals face a dual disadvantage: a material one relative to nomads, and an embodied one relative to longer-established former nomads or never nomads, with significantly lower levels of formal education, *Darija* fluency, and literacy that constrain social mobility and economic integration in settled contexts (educaiton figure or graph). As our data show, these gaps narrow with time spent in the community but will likely take generations to fully close (graph education over gen). Our findings suggest that policy efforts are likely to be most effective when directed at reducing the costs of voluntary sedentarisation rather than either promoting or discouraging the transition itself: e.g. by ensuring that settled communities have access to functional infrastucture; providing literacy classes and adequate educational programs to recently settled families; extending civil registration, documentation, education and healthcare access to nomadic populations still on the move; and, critically, preserving the conditions under which voluntary nomadism remains a viable choice for those who prefer it. Policies protecting traditional pasture routes and grazing rights,for instance, would help ensure that sedentarisation reflects genuine preference rather than the foreclosure of alternatives [@manzano2021HolisticUnderstandingPastoralism; @kratli2010LivingUncertaintyIntelligent]. 

### A window into past subsistence transitions
The transition from nomadic pastoralism to settled life has received comparatively little attention in the evolutionary social science of subsistence change, where theoretical and empirical work has focused primarily on the Neolithic forager-to-farmer transition [@bocquet-appel2011AgriculturalDemographicTransition; @richerson2001WasAgricultureImpossible; @larson2014EvolutionAnimalDomestication]. Our findings both parallel and depart from this literature in instructive ways. Like the forager-to-farmer transition, sedentarisation in our data appears to be driven by a combination of environmental push factors and the pull of new socioeconomic opportunities rather than by either alone, involves genuine welfare trade-offs and entails a fundamental reorganisation of social life. Where our findings depart most clearly from the broader sendentarisation literature is our knowledge about the degree of intentionality involved in the transition. Whether prehistoric foragers weighted the long-run consequences of sedentarisation is unknown; but our participants demonstrably did, weighing the freedoms of mobile life against the amenities of the village, accumulating capital over years or decades, consulting family members, and making decisions understood to be consequential and often irreversible. Our study illustrate how ongoing subsistence transitions provide a rare opportunity to examine the behavioural and motivational dynamics of subsistence change, including the role of social networks, intergenerational investment, and life-course timing. In this sense, they are not merely policy problems but living experiments in the dynamics of human subsistence change, with implications for our understanding of how and why people have repeatedly, across history, exchanged mobility for settlement.

### Limitations and future directions
Several limitations of this study should be noted. First, the cross-sectional design precludes causal inference about the consequences of settlement: observed differences between groups may partly reflect pre-existing differences in individual characteristics, household resources, or environmental conditions rather than the effects of the transition itself. Second, our sample of nomadic participants was recruited by convenience, a practical necessity given the logistical challenges of accessing mobile populations and funding limitations, and may not be representative of the broader nomadic population in the region, about whom systematic demographic data are inexistant. In particular, although we tried to travel to remote areas to find families we cannot discount the possibility that our sample selected for less isolated, more market integrated families. Third, although we draw on ethnographic knowledge and insights from local informants to triangulate and contextualize our quantitative findings, self-reported data on income, assets, and wellbeing remain subject to recall and social desirability biases. We are cautiously confident in the quality of our data, given the depth of our engagement with the study community over multiple field seasons and the independent corroboration provided by ethnographic observation, but we acknowledge this as a constraint. Future data will allow us to track changes in socioeconomic status, health outcomes (e.g.our ethnographic observations suggest that chronic disease burden is much elevated in the study village relative to the nomadic population), children's development and network structure over time, addressing some of the inferential limitations of the current cross-sectional design and giving us a better idea about the socioeconmic and health costs and benefits of sedentarisation. In doing so, we hope to continue to contribute to both our understanding of subsistence transitions and the evidence base for policies that affect some of the world's most vulnerable populations. 

## Conclusion
The sedentarisation of nomadic pastoralists is among the most consequential livelihood transitions occurring in the contemporary world, yet it has received remarkably little systematic attention from evolutionary social scientists. This study offers one of the first mixed-methods accounts of voluntary sedentarisation as it is actually experienced — documenting not only the structural pressures that make nomadic life increasingly difficult to sustain, but the deliberate, forward-looking logic through which individuals and families navigate the transition on their own terms.
Our findings converge on a picture of sedentarisation that is neither the crisis-driven last resort nor the straightforward modernization upgrade that dominant narratives suggest. It is, instead, a protracted, capital-intensive process of life-course reallocation — one oriented as much toward the next generation as toward the present, enabled by community networks rather than market integration, and chosen despite real costs by the large majority of those who undergo it. Our data suggest the barriers to successful settlement are material and embodied rather than motivational: recently settled individuals start at a significant disadvantage in terms of relative socioecomic standing, education, and cultural capital, and closing that gap takes time. These findings have implications that extend beyond the Moroccan context. As climate change continues to erode the economic viability of pastoral mobility across the arid and semi-arid zones of Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia, the question of how to support voluntary sedentarization equitably — without coercing it and without foreclosing the alternative — becomes increasingly urgent. They also speak to longstanding questions in human evolutionary science about why, repeatedly and across history, mobile populations have chosen to settle. Studying that process while it is still ongoing, and with the behavioral and motivational resolution that living populations afford, may be one of the most productive windows we have into the deep history of human subsistence change.

##### Funding Statement


##### Competing Interests

## References

:::{#refs}
:::

## Appendix {.appendix}

``` {r}
#| label: tbl-watfood-items
#| tbl-cap: "Item-level wording and response distribution for the water and food insecurity scales."
#| tbl-pos: "H"
watfood_info <- data.frame(
  Item = c("Water need", "Water interruption", "Changed plans (water)",
           "Fewer than 3 meals/day", "No food in house", "Hungry all day/night"),
  Wording = c(
    "In the past 6 months, have you worried that you wouldn't have enough water for your family's needs?",
    "In the past 6 months, has your source of water been interrupted or limited?",
    "Have you or your family ever had to change plans because of problems with your access to water?",
    "In the past 6 months, outside the month of Ramadan, have you eaten fewer than three meals a day?",
    "In the past 6 months, outside the month of Ramadan, have you had no food of any kind to eat in your house?",
    "In the past 6 months, outside the month of Ramadan, have you gone a whole day and night without eating?"
  ),
  col = c("water_need", "water_interruption", "water_change_plans",
          "fewer_meals", "no_food", "hungry_alldaynight"),
  has_always = c(TRUE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE)
)

likert_levs <- c("never", "rarely", "sometimes", "often", "always")
freq_counts <- t(sapply(watfood_info$col, function(cl) {
  table(factor(d[[cl]], levels = likert_levs))
}))
colnames(freq_counts) <- c("Never", "Rarely", "Sometimes", "Often", "Always")
watfood_tab <- cbind(watfood_info[, c("Item", "Wording")], as.data.frame(freq_counts))
watfood_tab$Always[!watfood_info$has_always] <- NA

watfood_tab %>%
  gt::gt(rowname_col = "Item") %>%
  gt::tab_row_group(label = "Food insecurity (FIES)", rows = 4:6) %>%
  gt::tab_row_group(label = "Water insecurity (HWISE)", rows = 1:3) %>%
  gt::row_group_order(groups = c("Water insecurity (HWISE)", "Food insecurity (FIES)")) %>%
  gt::sub_missing(missing_text = "—") %>%
  gt::cols_width(Wording ~ gt::px(230), everything() ~ gt::px(55)) %>%
  gt::tab_options(table.font.size = 8, table.width = gt::pct(100), data_row.padding = gt::px(2))
```

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[H]
\small
\centering
\rowcolors{2}{rowgray}{white}
\begin{tabular}{p{3.2cm}p{3.2cm}p{7.3cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Network} & \textbf{Dimension} & \textbf{Elicitation prompt} \\
\midrule
Labour & Daily labour & ``Who do you work with on a daily basis? Either in jnanate, with ksiba, on a construction site? Please name up to 10 people.'' \\
Labour & Childcare & ``Who helps you take care of your young children when you're busy?'' \\
Socialisation \& festivities & Couscous preparation & ``Who came to help prepare couscous? Name all the women who helped you.'' \\
Socialisation \& festivities & Friendship (women) & ``Please name up to 10 women with whom you like to sit outside and chat.'' \\
Socialisation \& festivities & Friendship (men) & ``Please name up to 10 men with whom you like to meet at the coffeeshop.'' \\
Socialisation \& festivities & Festivities (received) & ``Please name up to 10 women who have helped you and your household in the past with cooking or chores during a wedding or another celebration.'' \\
Socialisation \& festivities & Festivities (given) & ``Please name up to 10 women whom you have helped with cooking or chores during a wedding or another celebration.'' \\
Material support & Natural hazards (received) & ``Please name up to 10 people who provided you with any kind of help during that time.'' \\
Material support & Natural hazards (given) & ``Please name up to 10 people who you have helped during that time.'' \\
Material support & Loans (received) & ``Please name up to 10 people from whom you have borrowed, or from whom you or anyone in your household could borrow, over 200 dirhams.'' \\
Material support & Loans (given) & ``Please name up to 10 people who have borrowed over 200 dirhams from you or from other members of your household.'' \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Elicitation prompts used to construct the three social support networks.\label{tab-network-items}}
\end{table}
```

All four wealth and income models used weakly informative priors: Normal(0, 1) on group-level standard deviations and Normal(0, 5) on the intercept. Diagnostics are summarised in @tbl-mcmc-diag.

``` {r}
#| label: tbl-mcmc-diag
#| tbl-cap: "MCMC diagnostics for the Bayesian wealth and income models. Each model was run with 4 chains, 1,000 warmup and 1,000 sampling iterations (4,000 post-warmup draws total)."
#| tbl-pos: "H"
library(brms)
diag_models <- list(
  "Income, total effect" = inc_model_brm,
  "Income, full model"   = inc_model_brm_full,
  "Wealth, total effect" = wealth_model_brm,
  "Wealth, full model"   = wealth_model_brm_full
)
diag_tab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(diag_models), function(nm) {
  m  <- diag_models[[nm]]
  np <- nuts_params(m)
  data.frame(
    Model = nm,
    `Divergent transitions` = sum(np$Value[np$Parameter == "divergent__"]),
    `Parameters with Rhat > 1.01` = sum(rhat(m) >= 1.01, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Parameters with ESS ratio < 0.5` = sum(neff_ratio(m) < 0.5, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Parameters with ESS ratio < 0.1` = sum(neff_ratio(m) < 0.1, na.rm = TRUE),
    check.names = FALSE
  )
}))

diag_tab %>%
  gt::gt() %>%
  gt::tab_options(table.font.size = 8, table.width = gt::pct(100))
```

```{=latex}
\begin{figure}[h]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{figures/des_education_by_group.png}
  \caption{Years of schooling by group (nomads, former nomads, never nomads).}
  \label{fig:edu-group}
\end{figure}
```

```{=latex}
\begin{table}[ht]
\centering
\rowcolors{2}{rowgray}{white} % stripe every other row
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Variable} & \textbf{Outgoing ties} & \textbf{Incoming ties} & \textbf{Homophily} \\
\midrule
Age              & $\checkmark$ &             &             \\
Sex              & $\checkmark$ & $\checkmark$  & $\checkmark$ \\
Bone             & $\checkmark$ &             & $\checkmark$ \\
Household ID     &            &             & $\checkmark$ \\
External ties    & $\checkmark$ & $\checkmark$  &             \\
Years of schooling & $\checkmark$ & $\checkmark$ &             \\
Income (ln)      & $\checkmark$ & $\checkmark$  & $\checkmark$ \\
Wealth (ln)      & $\checkmark$ & $\checkmark$  & $\checkmark$ \\
Former Nomad     & $\checkmark$ & $\checkmark$  & $\checkmark$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\caption{Parameters included in ERGM models.\label{tab-ergm}}
\end{table}
```

``` {r}
#| label: full-table
#| tbl-cap: Full output from ERGMS. Nodefactor and nodecov refer to effects of variables on outgoing ties, nodeifactor and nodeicov on incoming ties. Nodematch and absdiff are estimates of homophily, and the "relmat" variables refer to relatedness. "Mutual" is an estimate of reciprocity, and "edges" is an intercept term (baseline logodds of ties)
tbl1 <- tbl_regression(mod_work, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95) |> 
    modify_header(label = "**Variable**", estimate = "**Odds Ratio**")
  
  tbl2 <- tbl_regression(mod_soc, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95)|>
    modify_header(estimate = "**Odds Ratio (**")
  
  tbl3 <- tbl_regression(mod_support, exponentiate = TRUE, conf.level = 0.95) |>
    modify_header(estimate = "**Odds Ratio**")
  
  final_table_full <- tbl_merge(
    tbls = list(tbl1, tbl2, tbl3),
    tab_spanner = c("**Labour (agriculture + childcare)**", "**Socialisation and festivities**", "**Material support (including loans)**")
  ) %>%
    remove_abbreviation() %>%
    # Remove CI footnote
    modify_caption("**Exponential Random Graph models of social networks (full output)**\\label{{tab-full}}")
  
  
  # Print
  final_table_full %>%
  as_gt() %>%
  gt::tab_options(table.font.size = 7,
                  table.width = gt::pct(105),
                  data_row.padding = gt::px(0),
                  table.align = "center"  ) %>%      # 
  gt::cols_width(
    label ~ gt::px(100), 
    everything() ~ gt::px(50)
  )
```
